text.title: mcp-gateway-composition Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for mcp-gateway-composition. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: When you put an MCP gateway in front of multiple servers, pick exactly one composition pattern per agent: (a) virtual server — gateway exposes a curated subset of upstream tools as one logical server; (b) federation — gateways consume other gateways in a tree (used for org-wide rollouts); (c) per-client visibility — gateway filters the tool list by user/role/scope. Mixing patterns inside one gateway instance creates tool-list cycles and ambiguous deny rules.
rule.rationale: Each pattern has a different failure mode if applied to the wrong shape: virtual server breaks for hierarchical orgs (no nesting), federation breaks for end-user filtering (no per-call identity), per-client visibility breaks for static catalogs (overhead with no benefit). One pattern per gateway keeps deny rules unambiguous.

rule.statement: If an agent connects to more than 5 MCP servers OR sees more than 25 tools in its catalog, route through a gateway. Below those thresholds, connect directly. Measure tool count from the agent's perspective — sum of unique tools across all connected servers, after any client-side filtering.
rule.rationale: Tool selection accuracy degrades sharply past ~25 flat tools (Anthropic, Composio guidance). 6 average MCP servers × ~10 tools each crosses both thresholds simultaneously. The gateway lets you lazy-load toolkits and trim the catalog per client without rewriting upstream servers.
